content.text: are a communication protocol that upgrades a standard HTTP connection into a persistent,

-- meaning both the client and the server can send messages to each other at any time, without either side needing to wait for the other to ask first. Once the connection is open, it stays open until one side explicitly closes it or the network drops.
The protocol is defined in RFC 6455 and is natively supported in every modern browser via the built-in
In agentic AI systems, WebSockets are the backbone of

: streaming LLM tokens to a chat UI token by token, pushing agent status events (tool calls, errors, completion) to a monitoring dashboard, and enabling bidirectional control so a human can interrupt a running agent mid-task.

LLMs are slow by nature -- a complex reasoning step can take 10 to 30 seconds. If you deliver the response only when the model finishes, users sit staring at a blank screen. WebSockets let you stream each token as it is generated, reducing
from tens of seconds to near-zero: the user sees the answer forming in real time.
Beyond token streaming, agents execute multi-step plans. Each step -- calling a search API, running code, reading a file -- can take seconds. WebSockets let the server push structured
as they happen, so the client can render a live trace of the agent's reasoning rather than a frozen spinner.

. A user must be able to inject new instructions, cancel a runaway tool call, or approve a high-stakes action mid-execution. WebSockets are the only standard transport that makes this possible without polling.
after the handshake, each WebSocket frame adds only 2 to 14 bytes of framing overhead versus the 200 to 800 bytes of HTTP headers on every polling request.
client and server send independently at any time -- no request/response round-trip. Ideal for cancel signals, human-in-the-loop approvals, and streaming side-channel data.
TCP connection stays open. No reconnect latency between messages. Especially valuable for long-running agent tasks that emit many events over minutes.
API is available in every modern browser with no dependencies. On the server, asyncio-native libraries like
or FastAPI's WebSocket support make server-side implementation straightforward.
messages arrive in the order they were sent -- TCP guarantees this. Token streams always appear in the correct sequence.
the connection starts as HTTP (port 80/443) and upgrades, so it passes through corporate proxies and firewalls that block non-HTTP ports.
Stateful connections are harder to scale:
each WebSocket connection binds to one server process. Horizontal scaling requires a pub/sub broker (Redis, Kafka) to route messages across servers.
idle open sockets consume file descriptors and memory. A server holding 50 000 idle connections needs careful resource tuning.
HTTP gives you status codes, caching, and retry logic for free. With WebSockets you build all message correlation yourself.
networks drop. Clients must implement exponential backoff reconnection and replay any missed events, which adds engineering effort.
CDN edge caches cannot cache WebSocket frames. If your use case is mostly read-heavy and infrequent, HTTP with caching may be cheaper.
if you only need to check a job status every 30 seconds, plain HTTP polling or SSE is simpler and sufficient.
. The client sends a standard HTTP/1.1 GET request with two special headers:
, plus a randomly generated 16-byte base64 key in
header derived from the client key using a fixed GUID and SHA-1. From this point on, the TCP socket is no longer HTTP.
. A frame consists of a 2 to 14 byte header (fin bit, opcode, masking key for client-to-server frames, payload length) followed by the payload. Opcodes include
(pong). Ping/pong frames are the protocol's built-in keep-alive mechanism.

A clean close is a two-step handshake: one side sends a close frame with an optional status code and reason, the other echoes a close frame back, and then the TCP connection tears down. Common close codes:
(abnormal -- network lost, never sent over the wire).

attrs.alt: WebSocket protocol flow: Client sends HTTP Upgrade then bidirectional WS frames; WebSocket Server manages connections, auth, routing, heartbeat, pub/sub; AI Agent/LLM emits token events and tool events back through the server to the client

content.text: A production WebSocket stack for an agentic AI system has three tiers. The
(browser, mobile app, or SDK) opens a single persistent connection per session. The

handles the protocol layer: authentication on upgrade, a registry mapping session IDs to socket objects, heartbeat logic to reap dead connections, and a pub/sub bridge (typically Redis) so messages can flow across multiple server instances. The
runs the model loop and emits structured JSON events -- token deltas, tool call starts, tool results, and a final done event -- which the WebSocket server fans out to the appropriate client.
streaming LLM tokens character by character. The user sees the response forming immediately instead of waiting for the full completion.
pushing live step-by-step traces -- each tool call, intermediate result, and thought step -- to an operator console.
the agent pauses before a destructive action and sends a confirmation request to the client; the human approves or denies via the same WebSocket.
agents on different machines communicate via a shared WebSocket message bus instead of polling a database.
multiple users editing a shared agent configuration or watching the same agent run simultaneously with live cursors.
fire-and-forget webhooks (use HTTP POST), infrequent status checks (use polling or SSE), or large file transfers (use direct object storage URLs).

library (pure asyncio) and FastAPI's built-in WebSocket support are the two most common choices in Python. The server-side pattern is: accept the upgrade, then loop reading frames and writing frames until the connection closes.

# Install dependencies
pip install websockets fastapi uvicorn anthropic

# client.py -- asyncio WebSocket client that prints tokens as they arrive

import asyncio
import json
import websockets


async def chat(prompt: str) -> None:
    uri = "ws://localhost:8000/ws/chat"
    async with websockets.connect(uri) as ws:
        # Send the user message
        await ws.send(json.dumps({"content": prompt}))

        # Receive streamed events until done
        async for raw in ws:
            event = json.loads(raw)
            if event["type"] == "token":
                print(event["text"], end="", flush=True)
            elif event["type"] == "done":
                print()
                usage = event["usage"]
                print(f"[{usage['input_tokens']} in / {usage['output_tokens']} out tokens]")
                break


asyncio.run(chat("Explain TCP/IP in three sentences."))
Rather than sending raw text, emit typed JSON events so the client can render different UI elements for different event kinds -- tokens, tool calls, and completion.

Claude.ai uses WebSockets to stream responses in its chat interface. When you send a message, the browser opens (or reuses) a WebSocket connection to the API edge layer. The edge layer forwards the request to the model backend and pipes back each token delta as a WebSocket frame. The JavaScript client appends each token to the DOM as it arrives -- which is why you see the response appearing word by word in real time rather than appearing all at once after a delay.
A comparable open-source example is the
(formerly OpenDevin) coding agent. Its frontend subscribes to a WebSocket channel that emits events for every agent action:
when the agent navigates a browser, and
when the task completes. The operator panel renders a real-time trace of everything the agent is doing.

Connection management tip: in production, keep a connection pool on the client side. Do not open a fresh WebSocket connection per user message -- reuse the same socket for the full session. Opening a new TCP connection plus TLS handshake for every prompt adds 100 to 300 ms of latency that defeats much of the benefit.
, an abrupt client close will crash your coroutine and leak any resources (open LLM streams, database transactions) it was holding.
on a disconnected socket raises an error. Always check
or catch the exception on send.
WebSocket servers are async. Calling a synchronous LLM SDK method inside an async handler blocks every other connection. Use streaming APIs or run blocking code in a thread pool with
the WebSocket Upgrade request is a plain HTTP request. Validate JWT or API key before calling
. Once accepted, any sender can write arbitrary messages to your agent.

load balancers (AWS ALB, nginx) silently close idle WebSocket connections after 60 to 600 seconds. Without periodic pings, long-running agent tasks lose their connection mid-stream. Send an application-level heartbeat every 30 seconds.
a slow client that cannot consume messages fast enough will cause the server's send buffer to grow without bound. Apply backpressure or drop non-critical messages when the buffer is full.
sends frames in cleartext. Use TLS (port 443) in every environment including staging.
check the Authorization header or a
query parameter in the Upgrade request. Reject with HTTP 401 before calling
). This makes client-side handling explicit and simplifies debugging.
reconnect with delays of 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 s (capped at ~60 s) plus jitter. Do not reconnect immediately in a tight loop.
Use a message ID for correlation:
in every client message and echo it back in every server response. This lets the client match streaming events to the originating prompt even when multiple requests are in flight.
in your disconnect handler, cancel any running LLM stream, release database connections, and remove the session from the connection registry.
A single asyncio server process can handle
tens of thousands of concurrent WebSocket connections

because each idle connection consumes almost no CPU -- just a file descriptor and a small memory buffer (~10 to 50 KB per socket). Active connections that are streaming LLM tokens are IO-bound and yield the event loop between chunks, so they scale well.
. If a client connects to server A and an agent running on server B needs to send it a message, server B cannot reach that socket directly. The standard solution is a
per session: any backend publishes to the channel; the server instance holding the socket subscribes and fans the message out.
Linux defaults to 1024 open file descriptors per process. Raise
set larger send/receive buffers if you stream large binary payloads. The uvicorn default is conservative.
if you emit thousands of tiny token events per second, the serialize/deserialize cost accumulates. Consider MessagePack or plain text frames for the hot token path.
(Python): pure asyncio WebSocket client and server library. Minimal, battle-tested, RFC 6455 compliant. Best for standalone WebSocket servers.
support in the routing layer. Ideal when you want REST and WebSocket endpoints in the same service.
: higher-level library that adds rooms, namespaces, automatic reconnection, and fallback to long-polling. Useful for broadcast semantics. Available for Python (
: the standard fan-out mechanism for multi-instance WebSocket scaling. Works with
: both terminate TLS and proxy WebSocket connections upstream. Configure
to 3600 s to avoid cutting off long agent runs.
: command-line WebSocket client for manual testing.
connections. Invaluable for debugging framing issues during development.
One-way token streams, no client-to-server needed
For most agentic AI web applications, the practical choice is between

. SSE is simpler -- it is just a long-lived HTTP response -- and is sufficient when the client only needs to receive the token stream without sending mid-stream messages. WebSocket is the right choice as soon as you need any of: interrupt/cancel signals, human approval prompts, or client-side context updates sent during a stream.
Unlike XHR, the browser sends cookies with WebSocket Upgrade requests. A malicious page on another origin can initiate a WebSocket connection to your server using the victim's cookies. Defend by
on the Upgrade request and rejecting connections from unexpected origins. Also require a short-lived token in the URL query string rather than relying on cookies alone.
WebSocket servers receive raw strings from clients. Never pass client-supplied content directly to an LLM without validation. Validate message structure -- correct
field, expected field types, length limits -- before processing. Apply prompt injection defenses if user content is passed into agent prompts.
A single WebSocket connection can send messages at arbitrary speed at zero reconnect cost. Apply
-- reject or drop messages that arrive faster than your policy allows and send an error event back to the client. For LLM-backed endpoints, also apply token-budget rate limits per user per minute.

with a valid certificate. For native mobile apps or CLI tools that carry sensitive agent actions (shell commands, file writes), consider certificate pinning to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks on the WebSocket stream.
Think of an HTTP request as a
: you write it, seal it, send it, and wait for a reply before you can write again. Think of a WebSocket as a

: once connected, both parties can speak and listen simultaneously without taking turns. The call setup (the HTTP Upgrade handshake) takes a moment, but after that the channel is free-flowing in both directions until one party hangs up.

For agents specifically: imagine the agent as a person working in a back room. Without WebSockets, you can only slide notes under the door and wait. With WebSockets, you have an open intercom -- the agent can narrate every step as it works, and you can shout new instructions or tell it to stop at any moment.

The HTTP Upgrade handshake is the only time either side follows HTTP rules. Everything after the 101 response is the WebSocket binary framing protocol running on the same TCP connection -- a completely different format.
WebSocket is a protocol upgrade, not a replacement:
it starts as HTTP, upgrades on the same TCP connection using the 101 Switching Protocols response, then becomes a binary framing channel.
both client and server can send at any time. SSE is receive-only; HTTP polling is send-then-wait. Only WebSocket supports real-time bidirectional control.
2 to 14 bytes of framing versus 200 to 800 bytes of HTTP headers. For high-frequency token streams this matters significantly.
validate tokens before calling accept(). Check the Origin header to prevent cross-site WebSocket hijacking.
Stateful connections require a pub/sub broker for horizontal scaling:
use Redis pub/sub to route messages across server instances. Each server subscribes to channels for its local sockets.
networks drop. Clients must handle reconnection gracefully and resume or replay any missed state.
field for every message makes the protocol self-documenting and keeps client-side rendering logic clean.
load balancers silently close idle sockets. A periodic heartbeat keeps the connection alive through long agent runs.
